claim: In the days before September 11, 2001, put-option trading on the parent companies of United Airlines (Sep 6) and American Airlines (Sep 10) ran approximately 25–100 times normal volumes. The SEC, FBI, and other agencies analyzed 9.5 million securities transactions across 103 companies in six industry groups across seven markets. The 9/11 Commission concluded that 95% of the UAL put activity was attributable to a single U.S.-based institutional investor with no ties to al-Qaeda (whose strategy also involved buying 115,000 shares of AMR on Sep 10), and that the bulk of unusual AMR put activity traced to a U.S.-based options newsletter that had recommended the trade in a Sep 9 fax to subscribers. The SEC's published conclusion: 'We have not developed any evidence suggesting that those who had advance knowledge of the September 11 attacks traded on the basis of that information.'

sources.label: National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States (9/11 Commission) — Staff Monograph, “The Financing of the 9/11 Plot” (Appendix A)
Snopes — “Were Stocks of Airlines Suspiciously Shorted Just Before 9/11?”
Unredacted (National Security Archive blog) — “Document Friday: 'Terrorist-Insider-Trading?' The SEC's Pre-September 11, 2001 Trading Review”
Wikipedia — “September 11 attacks advance-knowledge conspiracy theories” (put options section)
sources.quote: Exhaustive investigations by the Securities and Exchange Commission, FBI, and other agencies have uncovered no evidence that anyone with advance knowledge of the attacks profited through securities transactions.

sources.label: Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: This record documents BOTH halves: the unusual pre-9/11 trading activity is real and was investigated; the official conclusion (no foreknowledge trading) is also documented. Editorial significance: the reel's 'everyone made money that day' framing is undercut by the 9/11 Commission/SEC investigation; the reader should see both layers. Primary source is the 9/11 Commission's Terrorist Financing staff monograph, Appendix A.
